caveats: a: Semiconductor technicians are a process analogue, not PCB-specific labor evidence.
a: AI inspection evidence often concerns assembled boards as well as bare boards.
a: Rigid, flex, HDI, RF, prototype, and production facilities have different task mixes.
rho: No representative AI-adoption study for bare-board shops was found.
rho: Front-end engineering is easier to automate than closed-loop wet processing.
rho: Qualified defense and medical work requires auditable human control.
e: Repeat part orders are not contractual recurring revenue.
e: Some advertised full-service providers outsource bare-board fabrication.
e: Large advanced-board programs may not resemble LMM quick-turn economics.
s5: Cross-industry intentions are not observed PCB transactions.
s5: Stated plans may not produce a marketable plant.
s5: Distressed capacity may be available but require disproportionate reinvestment.
q: Capture is much lower in standard two-layer boards than HDI, flex, RF, or regulated work.
q: Large customers can dual-source after qualification.
q: Customers may directly claim benefits from faster automated quoting and DFM.
d5: Large-company growth may not reach LMM shops.
d5: Monthly book-to-bill data are volatile and not a five-year forecast.
d5: Demand can grow while obsolete facilities lose share.
o: The factor isolates AI-related obsolescence from ordinary offshore competition.
o: Legacy board technologies can become obsolete even while the industry grows.
o: Advanced substrate integration can shift value outside this NAICS.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include aerospace and defense contractors, data-center and networking OEMs, medical and industrial equipment makers, automotive suppliers, electronics manufacturing services providers, and hardware developers. Revenue is earned per custom board or production lot, with engineering review, nonrecurring setup, expedited turnaround, qualification, repeat releases, and time-to-market support creating service-like program economics.
lens.excluded_activities: PCB assembly without bare-board fabrication; semiconductor substrates classified elsewhere; pure design bureaus and brokers; commodity offshore-volume platforms without recurring engineering relationships; captive board plants; and large integrated manufacturers outside the LMM band.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market manufacturers of rigid, flex, rigid-flex, high-density interconnect, RF/microwave, and other bare printed circuit boards, emphasizing quick-turn prototypes, high-mix low-volume production, domestic or trusted manufacturing, engineering and CAM support, qualification, and repeat customer part numbers.

a.bridge: O*NET semiconductor-processing tasks are used as a process proxy for PCB photolithography, etching, chemical baths, inspection, computerized controls, and records, while electronics-industry evidence documents AI-enhanced optical inspection. These are translated into total PCB-fabricator labor-hour exposure. The proxy may overstate similarity to wafer fabs but understate CAM engineering, quoting, planning, documentation, and customer-service exposure.
caveats: Semiconductor technicians are a process analogue, not PCB-specific labor evidence.
AI inspection evidence often concerns assembled boards as well as bare boards.
Rigid, flex, HDI, RF, prototype, and production facilities have different task mixes.
a.change_evidence: Task-time data across domestic high-mix PCB shops, with measured AI effects in CAM, quoting, imaging, drilling, plating, inspection, test, maintenance, scheduling, quality, and customer support.
a.rationale: AI can automate design-rule and manufacturability review, CAM preparation, quoting, panelization, optical and electrical-test analysis, defect classification, process-window control, predictive maintenance, scheduling, documentation, and customer communication. Material loading, drilling, lamination, plating, wet chemistry, handling, destructive testing, maintenance, and approval of complex exceptions remain physical or expert-supervised.

d5.bridge: TTM's 2025 growth in data-center, networking, aerospace and defense demand and IPC's representative North American shipment, booking, and book-to-bill data are mapped to five-year real demand for the LMM domestic high-mix subset. They may overstate benefit if growth concentrates in advanced large plants or understate reshoring and secure-supply demand for smaller qualified shops.
caveats: Large-company growth may not reach LMM shops.
Monthly book-to-bill data are volatile and not a five-year forecast.
Demand can grow while obsolete facilities lose share.
d5.change_evidence: Independent 2026-2031 forecasts for North American PCB value and volume by rigid, flex, HDI, RF, prototype, defense, medical, data center, and automotive segments, including domestic capacity and imports.
d5.rationale: AI data centers, networking, aerospace and defense, medical electronics, automotive content, industrial automation, and secure domestic sourcing support complex PCBs. IPC reported strong year-to-date bookings and a book-to-bill ratio above one in March 2025 despite monthly shipment volatility. Risks include Asian price competition, customer inventory corrections, technology concentration, cyclicality, and heavy reinvestment.

e.bridge: TTM describes customized products, one-stop design, engineering and manufacturing, co-development, time-to-market support, and customer technology alignment, while quick-turn providers document expedited CAM review, prototype-to-production work, and repeat part numbers. These examples are mapped to the share of LMM firms with recurring service-like programs. The large-company proxy may overstate breadth, while public quick-turn marketing may overstate relationship depth.
caveats: Repeat part orders are not contractual recurring revenue.
Some advertised full-service providers outsource bare-board fabrication.
Large advanced-board programs may not resemble LMM quick-turn economics.
e.change_evidence: Establishment-level revenue by quick-turn, prototype, repeat production, NRE and engineering, standard commodity work, and regulated end market, with repeat-order and customer-retention rates.
e.rationale: High-mix domestic board houses become embedded through stack-up design, DFM, qualification, export-control compliance, rapid iterations, and repeat production releases. Commodity prototype and volume boards remain price-led transactional products, so eligibility is modeled near one half rather than across the whole industry.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 334412 ($2,223,297,039) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 334412 ($4,886,926,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2532 (margins.json[334]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Computers/Peripherals EBITDA/Sales 25.32%); revenue/employee $212,033 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 334412); class <5: 95 firms, avg revenue/firm $949,979 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $240,535 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 39 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,088,897 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $528,909 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 47 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,853,915 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $722,611 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 101 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,436,257 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,389,260 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $35,111,146 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,890,142 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $97,134,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $24,594,329 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 149

caveats: The factor isolates AI-related obsolescence from ordinary offshore competition.
Legacy board technologies can become obsolete even while the industry grows.
Advanced substrate integration can shift value outside this NAICS.
o.change_evidence: Commercial evidence that advanced packaging, additive manufacturing, cableless integration, or direct overseas sourcing broadly eliminates external bare-PCB fabrication for the target customer programs.
o.rationale: AI systems, data centers, vehicles, medical devices, and industrial electronics all require physical interconnects, and AI increases rather than removes leading-edge PCB complexity. Packaging integration, system-in-package designs, additive electronics, imported boards, or customer consolidation may displace specific domestic shops, but software cannot eliminate the physical interconnect function over five years.

q.bridge: TTM's co-development, advanced-technology, diverse end-market, and time-to-market model plus quick-turn service features are mapped to the fraction of AI-created value retained by LMM PCB firms. The large differentiated producer may overstate bargaining power; conversely, urgent prototype and trusted domestic programs can support premium capture at smaller firms.
caveats: Capture is much lower in standard two-layer boards than HDI, flex, RF, or regulated work.
Large customers can dual-source after qualification.
Customers may directly claim benefits from faster automated quoting and DFM.
q.change_evidence: Program-level margins and contracts showing NRE, expedite premiums, annual repricing, yield, scrap, claims, lead times, customer churn, and price retention after AI-enabled productivity changes.
q.rationale: Commodity board prices are globally transparent, so simple CAM and inspection savings face pass-through. Firms can retain more value from faster quotes and turns, improved yield on complex boards, fewer escapes, qualification, secure domestic supply, and design collaboration where switching or delay is costly.

caveats: No representative AI-adoption study for bare-board shops was found.
Front-end engineering is easier to automate than closed-loop wet processing.
Qualified defense and medical work requires auditable human control.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-plant before-and-after evidence on deployment cost, quote and CAM cycle times, labor, first-pass yield, scrap, rework, escapes, throughput, uptime, chemistry control, and payback by board technology and volume mix.
rho.rationale: PCB production is already digitally defined and inspection-intensive, making CAM, quoting, computer vision, anomaly detection, and scheduling accessible. Realization is constrained by legacy wet-process lines, varied customer files, scarce clean training data, false positives, export controls, cybersecurity, qualification, high-mix changeovers, environmental controls, and small engineering teams.

s5.bridge: Gallup's 2025 cross-industry survey of owner age and five-year disposition intentions is mapped to actionable acquisition availability among qualifying PCB fabricators. It may overstate availability because environmental permits, export controls, qualifications, and capital needs narrow buyers, or understate it because aging domestic board-shop owners may lack successors.
caveats: Cross-industry intentions are not observed PCB transactions.
Stated plans may not produce a marketable plant.
Distressed capacity may be available but require disproportionate reinvestment.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year transaction, closure, succession, and failed-sale data for independent North American PCB fabricators, including owner age, plant technology, permits, certifications, and customer programs.
s5.rationale: The frozen 149-firm LMM universe is large, but many entries may be subscale, commodity-exposed, obsolete, or misclassified. A financeable seller needs maintained imaging and wet-process assets, environmental compliance, skilled CAM and process staff, current certifications, repeat customers, and manageable reinvestment.

principal_driver: Complex, urgent, qualified PCB programs reward faster engineering and higher yield, making AI-enabled CAM and inspection directly valuable within sticky customer workflows.
principal_weakness: Commodity price competition and continuous technology reinvestment can overwhelm labor savings at undifferentiated or obsolete board shops.
